title: practical english usage - Part 317
content:
It was rather a disappointment.

I rather think we're going to lose. She rather likes gardening.

and nouns.

Rather can modify verbs (especially verbs that refer to thoughts and feelings) I've had rather a long day.

I think I'll put the heating on. It's rather cold.

Maurice speaks Russian rather well. People often think he is Russian.

‘ How was the film? ‘ Rather good -1 was surprised.* expected', ‘ more than was wanted', and similar ideas.

Rather is stronger than quite. It can suggest ‘ more than is usual’ , ‘ more than was 3   rather I quite enjoyed myself at your party.  The room was quite a mess.

Quite can also modify verbs and nouns.

He's lived in St Petersburg, so he speaks Russian quite well.

It's quite a difficult book -1 had trouble with it.

‘ How was the film? ‘ Quite good. You ought to go.* Quite (especially in British English) can suggest a higher degree than fairly.

I speak Russian fairly well - enough for everyday purposes.

‘ How was the film? ‘ Fairly good. Not the best one I've seen this year.’ she will not be very pleased.

degree: if you say that somebody is fairly nice or fairly clever, for example, he or Fairly generally modifies adjectives and adverbs. It does not suggest a very high 1  fairly ►    564-565.

American English, as is fairly. For more information about quite and rather, rather (in the senses discussed here) are more common in British than gradable descriptive words (►   189) in British English. Note also that quite and depends on the context. This entry gives a brief explanation of their use with These four modifiers differ somewhat in strength, though their exact force adverbs      of  degree 460 fairly,      quite,      rather       and   pretty: ►    549 facing,      opposite         and   in   front     of not I explained my problem to her. (     I explained her my problem.) xot (     Gan you explain mr . . .?) Can you explain to me how to get to your house?

After explain, we  use to before an indirect object.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  461  far and a long way For further information, see page 6.  College of Further Education Further (but not farther) can mean ‘ additional’ , ‘ extra’ , ‘ more advanced’ .

Edinburgh is farther/further away than York.

much less common in British English.

Further is not generally used in this sense in American English, and is becoming We   can use both farther and further to talk about distance. They  mean the same.

462 farther        and   fu  rther Much,  many and long (for time) are also more common in questions and negative sentences (►   165, 517).

Long ago, in a far country, there lived a woman who had seven sons.

formal and old-fashioned.

Far can be used as an adjective before a noun, meaning ‘ distant’ . This is rather 4 before     a  noun:   a  far    country You're far too young to get married.

She's far older than her husband.  This bike is by far the best.

superlatives and too.

Far is also used (in all kinds of clauses) to modify comparatives, 3 far    with    comparatives,         etc It's ready as far as I know. 'Any problems? 'OK so far.' 'She's gone far enough.' 'A bit too far.' However, far is normal in affirmative clauses with too, enough, as and so.

2 far    in  affirmative        clauses far from here.) The station is a long way from here. (More natural than The station is not We walked a long way. (     We walked far.) In affirmative clauses we  usually prefer a long way.

How far did you walk?  The youth hostel is not far from here.

Far is most common in questions and negative clauses.

1  far    in  questions       and   negatives 461 far    and   a  long     way I've pretty well finished.

Pretty well means ‘ almost’ .

'How's things? 'Pretty good. You OK?   You're driving pretty fast.

Pretty (informal) is like rather, but only modifies adjectives and adverbs.

vocabulary-  •  463  feel I feel (that) she's making a mistake.

That-c\a\ises are common.

He says he feels doubtful about the new plan.

I feel sure you're right, (     I'm feeling-sure-...) usually used in this case.
